posts.title: Landing in Namibia
posts.excerpt: There's a particular kind of stillness that settles over you the moment a plane's wheels touch down in a country you've only prayed about from a distance.
posts.content:
There's a particular kind of stillness that settles over you the moment a plane's wheels touch down in a country you've only prayed about from a distance. That's exactly what I felt as I landed in Namibia, a long way from Tana City Church in Antananarivo, Madagascar, but somehow already feeling like I was exactly where I was meant to be.

I didn't come to Namibia as a tourist. I came as a volunteer missionary, sent out by my home church family at Tana City Church, to serve for 3 months with Live 2:6, a discipleship-making program run by Jlife Africa. The name itself carries weight, a reminder that this year isn't about me. It's about walking alongside others, learning together, and growing as a disciple who makes disciples.
Leaving Madagascar wasn't easy. There were tears at the airport, prayers whispered over suitcases, and a church family standing behind me with a kind of love that travels further than any flight ever could. But there was also excitement, the quiet certainty that comes from knowing you've said yes to something bigger than yourself.

Stepping off the plane, everything felt both foreign and familiar. The wide skies, the dry warmth of the air, the unfamiliar rhythms of a new city, all of it reminded me that I was far from home. And yet, there was a welcome waiting for me too: new faces, new teammates, and the sense that I had just joined a much bigger story than my own.
Namibia is different from Madagascar in so many ways; the language, the landscape, the pace of life. But the mission is the same everywhere: to know Christ and to make Him known, one relationship, one conversation, one discipleship moment at a time.

I don't know yet exactly what this year will hold. New friendships, hard lessons, unexpected joys, homesickness, growth, probably all of it. But I do know this: I didn't come here by accident. I was sent, prayed over, and carried here by a church family that believed this was worth the sacrifice.
